title: AI & Technology Ethics
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Technology and Ethics
summary: Scripture provides principles for navigating technological power: the unique dignity of human beings as image-bearers, the stewardship of power, and the temptation to build towers that rival God.
body:
The Bible's first technology story is Babel. Humanity acquires the ability to build a tower to the heavens and uses it to make a name for themselves [verse:gen.11.4]. The sin is not building — it is building without reference to God, using power to achieve autonomy rather than to serve.

Every new technology amplifies human capacity, and Scripture insists that greater capacity demands greater responsibility. To whom much is given, much will be required [verse:luke.12.48]. Artificial intelligence, genetic engineering, and digital surveillance are not addressed by name, but the principles governing their use are clear: power is a stewardship, not an entitlement.

The imago Dei remains foundational [verse:gen.1.27]. Human beings are not reducible to data, algorithms, or productivity metrics. Any technology that treats people as inputs rather than image-bearers has departed from biblical ethics. The Sabbath commandment itself declares that human value is not determined by output [verse:exod.20.8-10].

Paul's framework of freedom applies: all things are lawful, but not all things are beneficial [verse:1cor.10.23]. Technology can serve human flourishing or undermine it. The question is not whether a technology can be built but whether it should be deployed, and whether its deployment honors or diminishes the people it affects.

Wisdom — the ability to apply knowledge rightly — is the Bible's answer to the problem of power. The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom [verse:prov.9.10]. Without that anchor, technological progress becomes the latest form of Babel: impressive construction with no reference to the Creator.

verses.annotation: The Tower of Babel — technology used for self-glorification rather than divine purpose.
Humanity as image-bearers — the dignity that technology must never diminish.
Greater capacity brings greater responsibility — power must be stewarded.
Not everything permissible is beneficial — a filter for technological deployment.
The fear of the Lord as the beginning of wisdom — the anchor for applying knowledge rightly.
Sabbath rest declares that human worth is not defined by productivity.
Justice, mercy, and humility — ethical principles that apply regardless of technological era.

scholars.quote: Keener reads the Babel narrative as establishing a recurring biblical pattern: human ingenuity divorced from divine accountability produces impressive achievements that ultimately serve pride rather than flourishing.

Fee argues that Paul's 'beneficial' test in 1 Corinthians 10 provides a durable ethical framework: the question is never merely what can be done but whether doing it serves love and builds up the community.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, 2014)

scholars.quote: Longman emphasizes that Proverbs defines wisdom not as intellectual achievement but as the skill of living rightly — a definition that makes it the essential complement to any advance in human capability.
